question_text: A standard-cell library is constrained so that only one universal gate family may be used to build an inverter, AND, and OR functions. Which gate family is sufficient by itself?

Option_C: kT / C

question_text: Which of the following describes the accurate matching behavior of a pair of identical resistors implemented on a silicon wafer layout?
Option_A: Matching accuracy depends exclusively on the sheet resistance value.
Option_B: Matching accuracy is optimized by routing matching resistors perpendicular to each other.
Option_C: Matching accuracy improves as width approaches the minimum sub-micron design rules.
Option_D: Matching accuracy improves with larger layout areas and closer physical proximity.

question_text: What is the primary engineering rationale for selecting a Chopper Stabilization architecture over an Auto-Zeroing architecture in low-frequency instrumentation amplifiers?
Option_A: Chopper stabilization modulates low-frequency noise to higher frequencies without causing noise aliasing (folding) down to DC.
Option_B: Chopper stabilization entirely avoids using any active clock signal lines.
Option_C: Chopper stabilization reduces the physical layout area of the amplifier by half.
Option_D: Chopper stabilization eliminates the need for any high-frequency filter elements.

question_text: In deep sub-micron CMOS nodes, how does reducing the supply voltage (VDD) affect analog design margins?
Option_A: It exponentially reduces the gate leakage currents through thin oxide dielectrics.
Option_B: It decreases voltage headroom, making it harder to keep cascoded transistors in saturation.
Option_C: It completely eliminates flicker noise from the active signal paths.
Option_D: It increases the intrinsic gain (gm * ro) of short-channel transistors.

question_text: What is the impact of a significant Left-Half-Plane (LHP) zero introduced into the feedback loop of an operational amplifier?
Option_A: It adds an immediate 90 degree phase lag, accelerating loop instability.
Option_B: It attenuates high-frequency gain signals, acting like a low-pass filter element.
Option_C: It provides positive phase shift (lead), which can improve the phase margin.
Option_D: It limits the low-frequency open-loop DC gain of the input differential stage.

question_text: Which of the following mechanisms causes the non-zero output conductance (1/ro) in short-channel MOSFETs, beyond standard channel length modulation?
Option_A: Grown junction oxide tunneling leakage fields
Option_B: Pelgrom geometric variation effects
Option_C: Bipolar carrier recombination lifetime parameters
Option_D: Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)

question_text: In a matched transistor array, dummy devices are placed at the array edges. What fabrication-related purpose do these dummy devices serve?
Option_A: To ensure uniform etching and photolithography profiles for the critical core matching elements
Option_B: To serve as alternative backup connections if primary devices fail electrostatically
Option_C: To increase the parasitic routing capacitance around sensitive amplifier nodes
Option_D: To route high-current digital signal paths through vacant layout areas

question_text: A PLL has settled after acquisition. Which observation best indicates that the loop is locked?
Option_A: The loop filter output voltage begins oscillating symmetrically between ground and the supply rails.
Option_B: The frequency of the feedback signal matches the reference signal exactly, with a constant phase difference.
Option_C: The charge pump injects maximum current into the system loop continuously.
Option_D: The VCO frequency drops to zero hertz, eliminating phase noise.

question_text: An ideal ADC converts a continuous input into a finite number of output codes. What is the fundamental origin of quantization noise?
Option_A: Thermal noise generated by the sampling switches' resistance
Option_B: The finite rejection ratio of supply ripples through reference voltage nodes
Option_C: The rounding error that occurs when mapping a continuous analog signal onto a discrete set of digital codes
Option_D: Substrate leakage current across the comparator input stages

question_text: A Miller-compensated amplifier suffers from a destabilizing feedforward path through the compensation capacitor. Which compensation approach uses a buffer to remove the RHP zero?
Option_A: Simple shunt RC peak compensation
Option_B: Dominant pole filtering compensation without feedback structures
Option_C: Cross-coupled positive feedback neutralization layout style
Option_D: Ahuja compensation or voltage/current buffer Miller compensation

question_text: In a continuous-time delta-sigma modulator, what is the loop filter primarily designed to do to quantization noise?
Option_A: It shapes quantization noise, shifting it from low frequencies to high frequencies.
Option_B: It acts as a high-speed sample-and-hold circuit for input tracking.
Option_C: It dampens the master clock signal to prevent clock jitter.
Option_D: It converts the multi-bit digital output code back into an analog current.

question_text: A bulk CMOS chip must be protected from latch-up during transient current injection. What mechanism causes latch-up, and what layout method helps prevent it?
Option_A: Dielectric breakdown of the gate oxide; prevented by adding series resistors to gates
Option_B: Parasitic SCR turn-on; prevented by utilizing guard rings and minimizing substrate resistance
Option_C: Electromigration of metal paths; prevented by widening supply lines
Option_D: Channel hot-carrier injection; prevented by increasing channel lengths

question_text: An op amp with 60 dB DC gain has real poles at 1 MHz, 10 MHz, and 50 MHz. What is the likely result when it is used as a unity-gain feedback buffer without compensation?
Option_A: The circuit will be perfectly stable with a phase margin exceeding 60 degrees.
Option_B: The poles will cancel each other out at unity gain, linearizing the amplifier.
Option_C: The circuit will be unstable and oscillate because the loop gain is much greater than 1 before the phase reaches -180 degrees.
Option_D: The open-loop DC gain will drop to 0 dB instantly due to negative feedback loading.

question_text: For a medium-resolution data converter, why is a SAR ADC often preferred over a flash ADC?
Option_A: Higher sampling speed, completing conversions within a single clock cycle
Option_B: Absolute immunity to clock jitter and thermal noise errors
Option_C: Elimination of the need for an input sample-and-hold circuit
Option_D: Significantly smaller layout area and lower power consumption for medium-to-high resolutions

question_text: A low-noise CMOS amplifier must reduce low-frequency 1/f noise at the input. Which input-device choice is usually prioritized?
Option_A: Use PMOS input transistors with large channel areas (W x L).
Option_B: Minimize transistor width while maximizing channel length to limit bias currents.
Option_C: Operate the input pair deep in the triode region with minimal drain voltage.
Option_D: Replace the input transistors with small unsiliciated poly resistors.

question_text: What is the total integrated thermal noise power (mean square noise voltage) across a basic parallel RC network connected to an open thermal system?
Option_A: vn² = 4kTR
Option_B: vn² = kT / C
Option_C: vn² = 4kTRB
Option_D: vn² = kT / (R · C)

question_text: In a discrete-time switched-capacitor circuit clocked at frequency Fs, a capacitor C switched between two discrete voltage nodes behaves globally as an equivalent resistor. What is its effective resistance value (Req)?
Option_A: Req = C · Fs
Option_B: Req = 1 / (C · Fs)
Option_C: Req = ln(2) / (C · Fs)
Option_D: Req = Fs / C

question_text: A 12-bit Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC utilizes a binary-weighted capacitive DAC. If the total capacitance of the array is C_tot, what is the value of the Least Significant Bit (LSB) capacitor?
Option_A: C_tot / 12
Option_B: C_tot / 2048
Option_C: C_tot / 4096
Option_D: C_tot / 4095

question_text: How does Flicker Noise (1/f noise) in MOSFET devices scale with transistor geometry, and which channel type typically exhibits lower flicker noise in standard bulk CMOS?
Option_A: Proportional to area (W·L); NMOS is quieter than PMOS.
Option_B: Inversely proportional to area (1 / (W·L)); PMOS typically exhibits lower flicker noise than NMOS.
Option_C: Independent of device length (L); NMOS is quieter due to higher mobility.
Option_D: Inversely proportional to oxide thickness only; PMOS and NMOS show identical performance.

question_text: What is the primary purpose of utilizing 'Non-Overlapping Clocks' (Phi1 and Phi2) in switched-capacitor circuits?
Option_A: To accelerate the operational charging speed of internal analog nodes.
Option_B: To prevent the simultaneous conduction of sampling switches, which would cause direct charge leakage between separate analog nodes.
Option_C: To shape the quantization noise profile into high-frequency spectrum bands.
Option_D: To minimize the physical injection of substrate currents into the active digital core.

question_text: Which design technique effectively reduces digital switching noise coupling into sensitive analog nodes through a common silicon substrate?
Option_A: Routing high-speed digital lines directly parallel to sensitive analog input lines.
Option_B: Utilizing guard rings connected to clean, dedicated supply lines, and isolating analog blocks inside deep N-wells.
Option_C: Tying analog and digital ground networks together globally using a long, high-impedance metal trace.
Option_D: Increasing the input drive strength of all nearby digital clock buffers.

question_text: An engineer evaluates an ADC and reports an Effective Number of Bits (ENOB) of 9.2 bits. If the target signal bandwidth is 2 MHz, what is the calculated Sinad (Signal-to-Noise and Distortion) value?
What is the primary function of 'Miller Compensation' in a standard two-stage CMOS operational amplifier?
Option_A: To increase the high-frequency open-loop voltage gain.
Option_B: To split the pole frequencies by moving the dominant pole lower and the non-dominant pole higher, ensuring circuit stability.
Option_C: To eliminate the right-half-plane zero created by parasitic path bypass.
Option_D: To lower the total static power dissipation of the output stage.

question_text: What is the difference between SNR and SNDR (SINAD) in ADC characterisation?
Option_A: SNR includes only thermal noise; SNDR adds harmonic distortion components, giving a more complete measure of dynamic performance
Option_B: SNR and SNDR are identical metrics with different names
Option_C: SNDR measures only distortion; SNR measures only noise
Option_D: SNR applies to DACs; SNDR applies to ADCs

question_text: Explain DNL and INL in the context of ADC/DAC linearity.
Option_A: DNL measures total offset error; INL measures gain error
Option_B: DNL (Differential Non-Linearity) is the deviation of each code step from the ideal 1-LSB step; INL (Integral Non-Linearity) is the cumulative deviation of the transfer curve from the ideal straight line
Option_C: DNL and INL both measure harmonic distortion at different frequencies
Option_D: INL is the per-code error; DNL is the worst-case total error across all codes

question_text: What is a switched-capacitor circuit, and what key advantage does it have over a resistor-based circuit in CMOS?
Option_A: A circuit using MOSFET capacitors to store charge; advantage is higher bandwidth
Option_B: A circuit where switches and capacitors emulate a resistor; advantage is that the effective resistance is set by capacitor ratio and clock frequency, both of which are highly accurate in CMOS
Option_C: A circuit using MOS switches to bypass capacitors; advantage is reduced area
Option_D: A circuit using switching regulators; advantage is higher power efficiency

question_text: What is a Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit, and why is it critical in ADC design?
Option_A: A circuit that averages the input signal over multiple clock cycles
Option_B: A circuit that samples the analog input at a defined instant and holds it constant during the ADC conversion, preventing errors from input signal movement
Option_C: A circuit that stores digital codes between conversion cycles
Option_D: A circuit that holds the reference voltage stable during conversion

question_text: What is CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio) and why does it matter for analog circuits?
Option_A: The ratio of differential-mode gain to common-mode gain; high CMRR means the circuit rejects noise that appears identically on both inputs
Option_B: The ratio of signal bandwidth to noise bandwidth; higher CMRR indicates a wider useful bandwidth
Option_C: The ratio of output swing to supply voltage; higher CMRR means better rail-to-rail performance
Option_D: The ratio of input impedance to output impedance; affects matching

question_text: In a PLL, what is the role of the VCO and the Loop Filter?
Option_A: VCO generates the reference clock; Loop Filter digitises the phase error
Option_B: VCO produces an output frequency proportional to a control voltage; the Loop Filter smooths the phase detector output to produce a stable DC control voltage and sets loop bandwidth/stability
Option_C: Loop Filter multiplies the reference frequency; VCO divides it down
Option_D: VCO detects phase error; Loop Filter amplifies it for the frequency divider

question_text: What is the purpose of an anti-aliasing filter (AAF) placed before an ADC?
Option_A: To increase the ADC sampling rate
Option_B: To attenuate signal components above fs/2 before sampling, preventing them from folding (aliasing) into the desired signal band

Option_D: To reduce power consumption of the ADC by limiting its input bandwidth

question_text: What is oversampling in the context of ADCs, and what SNR benefit does it provide?
Option_A: Sampling faster than required offers no inherent SNR benefit but reduces latency
Option_B: Oversampling by a factor of M spreads quantisation noise over a wider bandwidth; decimation filtering recovers approximately 3 dB (0.5 bit) of SNR for every doubling of the oversampling ratio
Option_C: Oversampling doubles the ADC resolution directly
Option_D: Oversampling improves INL by averaging out non-linearity across codes

question_text: What is a current mirror, and what is the key design parameter that determines its accuracy?
Option_A: A circuit that replicates a supply current to multiple loads; accuracy depends on supply voltage
Option_B: A circuit that copies a reference current to one or more output branches; accuracy is primarily determined by transistor matching (W/L ratio, Vth mismatch), output impedance, and channel-length modulation
Option_C: A circuit that converts voltage to current; accuracy depends on resistance tolerance

question_text: What is a Sigma-Delta (Delta-Sigma) ADC, and what technique does it use to achieve high resolution?
Option_A: A flash ADC that uses many comparators in parallel; resolution is set by the number of comparators
Option_B: An ADC that uses oversampling and noise shaping to push quantisation noise out of the signal band, followed by digital decimation filtering to achieve high resolution at low bandwidth
Option_C: A successive-approximation ADC that uses a sigma modulator to reduce conversion time
Option_D: A pipeline ADC that uses delta coding between stages to reduce power

question_text: What is slew rate in an op-amp, and what causes it?
Option_A: The maximum frequency at which the op-amp can operate; caused by parasitic capacitance
Option_B: The maximum rate of change of the output voltage (V/us); caused by the limited current available to charge the internal compensation capacitor
Option_C: The minimum output impedance achievable; caused by transistor sizing
Option_D: The input offset voltage drift; caused by temperature coefficient mismatch

question_text: What is the purpose of a charge pump in a PLL?
Option_A: To supply power to the VCO during lock
Option_B: To convert the phase detector's UP/DOWN pulses into a proportional analog current that charges/discharges the loop filter capacitor, generating the VCO control voltage
Option_C: To double the reference clock voltage for the frequency divider
Option_D: To filter thermal noise from the reference oscillator

question_text: What is the difference between a SAR ADC and a Flash ADC in terms of speed, resolution, and area?
Option_A: SAR and Flash have identical speed/area; they differ only in power consumption
Option_B: Flash ADC uses 2^N-1 comparators for N-bit resolution, giving very high speed (1 cycle) but large area/power; SAR ADC uses binary search with one comparator over N cycles, achieving moderate speed with much smaller area and lower power
Option_C: SAR uses parallel comparators; Flash uses a single comparator iteratively
Option_D: Flash ADC is used only for audio; SAR is used only for RF applications

question_text: In a Multi-bit Pipelined ADC architecture, what is the role of the 'Sub-DAC' and 'Residue Amplifier' inside an intermediate stage?
Option_A: To completely digitize the input signal into its final binary code.
Option_B: To convert the local stage's coarse digital decision back to an analog value, subtract it from the input signal, and amplify the remaining residue for the next stage.
Option_C: To isolate the digital outputs from clock jitter.
Option_D: To provide low-pass filtering to eliminate flicker noise components.

question_text: What is the primary cause of 'Charge Injection' errors in MOS switch circuits, and how does it scale with device size?
Option_A: Thermal noise storage in the channel; scales only with temperature.
Option_B: The release of mobile channel inversion charges into the source/drain nodes when the switch turns off; scales proportionally with switch gate area (W·L).
Option_C: Substrate leakage currents; scales inversely with gate oxide thickness.

question_text: In a SAR ADC, why is capacitor mismatch in the binary-weighted DAC a critical design concern?
Option_A: It increases the sampling frequency beyond the Nyquist limit.
Option_B: It causes reference voltage ripple only during reset.
Option_C: It creates incorrect DAC step sizes, leading to INL/DNL errors and possible conversion inaccuracies.
Option_D: It eliminates the need for comparator offset calibration.

question_text: If a pipelined or SAR ADC exhibits a recurrent localized Differential Nonlinearity (DNL) violation that drops exactly to -1 LSB at a major code transition, what does this indicate about the converter's transfer characteristic?
Option_A: The converter is operating at maximum ideal performance.
Option_B: The converter exhibits a 'Missing Code' error due to non-monotonicity or severe component mismatch.
Option_C: The converter's thermal noise floor is lower than the quantization step size.
Option_D: The input anti-aliasing filter has an improper cutoff frequency.

question_text: In a standard UVM testbench component execution life cycle, during which execution phase are structural components instantiated using the factory mechanism?
Option_A: connect_phase
Option_B: run_phase
Option_C: build_phase
Option_D: end_of_elaboration_phase

question_text: Why must a 'virtual interface' be utilized inside a SystemVerilog class-based verification component (like a driver) rather than instantiating a regular interface directly?
Option_A: Direct interface instantiations inside dynamic classes violate compiler syntax because classes cannot contain physical hardware net models directly.
Option_B: Virtual interfaces run twice as fast during execution due to compiler optimization.
Option_C: Virtual interfaces automatically filter out meta-stability from clock domain cross routes.
Option_D: Regular interfaces can only be instantiated inside the scoreboard component.

question_text: Unlike other standard UVM phases, why is the UVM 'run_phase' unique in its internal execution structure?
Option_A: It is the only phase implemented as a task rather than a function, which allows it to consume simulation time.
Option_B: It is executed before the build_phase is reached.
Option_C: It cannot be overridden or modified by the user.
Option_D: It runs entirely outside the main simulator compilation process.

question_text: What is the primary purpose of utilizing the 'solve...before' constraint construct during class randomization operations in SystemVerilog?
Option_A: To accelerate constraint execution speed by a factor of two.
Option_B: To alter the probability distribution of randomized variables without narrowing the absolute space of legal solution combinations.
Option_C: To force a deterministic value assignment, which completely disables random generation.
Option_D: To guarantee that an output file is opened before data logging begins.

question_text: In SystemVerilog tasks and functions, what is the core operational distinction between an argument passed as 'input' vs. an argument passed as a 'ref'?
Option_A: 'input' works only for real data types; 'ref' works only for virtual structures.
Option_B: 'input' copies the variable value into the routine at the call step; 'ref' passes a direct reference pointer to the original variable, allowing any modifications inside the routine to immediately change the caller's scope variables without copy overhead.
Option_C: 'ref' blocks the compilation if the argument is not explicitly a signed constant integer.
Option_D: There is no difference; they are completely interchangeable syntax mechanisms.

question_text: What is the primary difference between a 'Concurrent Assertion' and an 'Immediate Assertion' in SystemVerilog Assertions (SVA)?
Option_A: Immediate assertions check complex multi-cycle temporal sequences; concurrent assertions evaluate instantaneous procedural conditions.
Option_B: Immediate assertions execute procedurally like standard 'if' conditions within an execution block; concurrent assertions evaluate continuous expressions over time, relying on a clock edge and formal timing parameters.
Option_C: Immediate assertions can only be defined inside UVM scoreboards.
Option_D: Concurrent assertions automatically fix any detected timing skew in hardware routes.

question_text: What is SystemVerilog UVM (Universal Verification Methodology) and what problem does it solve?
Option_A: A synthesis flow that converts SystemVerilog to gate-level netlists
Option_B: A standardized, reusable class-based verification framework built on SystemVerilog OOP that provides a structured environment (driver, monitor, scoreboard, sequencer) to accelerate testbench development and promote component reuse across projects
Option_C: A formal verification language used to express safety properties
Option_D: A low-power design methodology for multi-voltage SoC verification

question_text: What is functional coverage, and how does it differ from code coverage?
Option_A: Functional coverage and code coverage are identical metrics
Option_B: Code coverage measures which RTL lines/branches were executed; functional coverage measures whether the verification plan's scenarios and corner cases have been exercised, regardless of which code lines ran
Option_C: Functional coverage measures timing path coverage; code coverage measures logic gate coverage
Option_D: Functional coverage is only applicable to formal verification; code coverage applies to simulation

question_text: What is constrained-random verification (CRV), and why is it preferred over directed testing for complex designs?
Option_A: CRV generates only directed test cases from a fixed script; preferred because it is deterministic
Option_B: CRV uses a constraint solver to automatically generate random stimulus satisfying design protocol constraints, exploring a much wider input space than manually written directed tests, while still producing legal, meaningful transactions
Option_C: CRV applies random values without constraints; preferred because it maximizes coverage speed
Option_D: CRV is a formal method that randomly selects properties to prove

question_text: What is Clock Domain Crossing (CDC) verification, and what kind of bugs does it catch?
Option_A: Verifying that all flip-flops share the same clock frequency
Option_B: Analyzing signals that cross between asynchronous clock domains to detect metastability risks, missing synchronizers, improper multi-bit bus crossings (data coherency issues), and incorrect synchronization protocols
Option_C: Checking that clock skew across the chip meets timing closure requirements
Option_D: Verifying that clock gating cells are correctly placed in the clock tree

question_text: What is a UVM scoreboard, and what role does it play in a self-checking testbench?
Option_A: A scoreboard is a coverage database that tracks which tests have been run
Option_B: A scoreboard receives transactions from monitors on the DUT's inputs and outputs, predicts expected output using a reference model, and compares predictions against actual DUT outputs to automatically flag functional mismatches
Option_C: A scoreboard is a sequence library that prioritizes which test scenarios to run next
Option_D: A scoreboard is a log file generated by the simulator recording all signal transitions

question_text: What is gate-level simulation (GLS), and why is it performed after synthesis?
Option_A: GLS simulates the RTL before synthesis to establish a golden reference
Option_B: GLS simulates the post-synthesis netlist (with or without SDF back-annotation) using actual gate delays to verify that synthesis did not introduce functional bugs, X-propagation issues, or timing violations that RTL simulation would miss
Option_C: GLS is a formal technique that proves the synthesized netlist is equivalent to the RTL
Option_D: GLS verifies the physical layout against the schematic (LVS)

question_text: What is X-propagation in simulation and why is it a concern?
Option_A: X represents a don't-care value used to optimize logic; it is beneficial for area reduction
Option_B: X (unknown) propagates through logic when signals are uninitialized or in contention. X-optimism in RTL simulation (masking of X values) can hide real bugs that would cause unpredictable behavior in silicon or GLS
Option_C: X-propagation refers to crosstalk between parallel signal wires causing unknown values
Option_D: X-propagation is an STA term for unclocked path analysis

question_text: What is the Register Abstraction Layer (RAL) in UVM, and how does it benefit SoC verification?
Option_A: RAL is a physical layer model for DDR memory interfaces
Option_B: RAL provides a high-level, object-oriented model of the DUT's register map, allowing testbench code to read/write registers using named fields rather than raw addresses, and enables access via frontdoor (bus transactions) and backdoor (direct HDL path) mechanisms
Option_C: RAL is a tool that automatically generates RTL register files from a specification
Option_D: RAL is the UVM component responsible for generating AXI bus transactions

question_text: What is power-aware verification, and what standard is commonly used to describe power intent?
Option_A: Power-aware verification checks IR drop in the power grid using SPICE simulation
Option_B: Power-aware verification simulates the design with its power management behavior enabled (power domains, isolation, retention, level shifting). The IEEE 1801 UPF standard describes power intent, and simulators use it to insert behavioral models of isolation/retention cells and check correct power sequencing.
Option_C: Power-aware verification is performed only at transistor level using analog simulators
Option_D: Power-aware verification uses formal methods to prove that clock gating saves power

question_text: What is an SVA sequence, and how does it differ from an SVA property?
Option_A: A sequence and a property are identical constructs in SVA
Option_B: An SVA sequence defines a series of Boolean conditions over time using temporal operators (##, [*], throughout, etc.); a property wraps a sequence with a directive such as 'always', 'eventually', or implication (|->) to express a complete verification intent that can be checked with assert, assume, or cover
Option_C: A sequence is used only in formal verification; a property is used only in simulation
Option_D: A property defines clock domains; a sequence defines data values

question_text: How is an associative array defined in SystemVerilog, and what is its primary memory management advantage over a standard dynamic array?
Option_A: It is allocated via a static fixed size; it blocks run-time out-of-bounds corruption.
Option_B: It is indexed via a key data type (e.g., string or sparse address ranges) and allocates memory sparsely on-demand, which avoids reserving massive blocks of contiguous memory for wide, unpopulated spaces.
Option_C: It forces elements to match a circular FIFO behavior automatically.
Option_D: It requires zero compiler memory footprint because it is stored directly on the physical host hardware disc cache.

question_text: What is the purpose of the 'phase_ready_to_end' function in a UVM component, and how does it interact with the objection mechanism?
Option_A: It immediately terminates the simulator when any component drops its last objection.
Option_B: It is a callback executed automatically when all objections for the current phase are dropped, allowing components to delay phase termination to perform clean-up or wait for a small delay (drain time).
Option_C: It forces the build_phase to restart if a structural configuration error is detected
Option_D: It converts 4-state simulation logic variables into 2-state logic variables before data log writing.

question_text: What is the key behavioral difference between SystemVerilog fork-join execution structures block variations 'fork...join_any' and 'fork...join_none'?
Option_A: 'join_any' blocks execution until all parent tasks finish; 'join_none' blocks infinitely.
Option_B: 'join_any' stalls the main execution thread until ANY single spawned background process completes; 'join_none' allows the main execution thread to continue immediately without waiting for any background process to start or finish.
Option_C: 'join_any' allows only one process to launch; 'join_none' disables all internal threads.
Option_D: They behave identically except that 'join_none' suppresses compiler optimization warnings.

question_text: Which communication mechanism is standard in UVM to decouple data passing transaction packets between separate components like a driver and its controlling sequencer?
Option_A: SystemVerilog Virtual Mailboxes
Option_B: TLM Ports and Exports (Transaction-Level Modeling)
Option_C: Global software arrays
Option_D: Hardware wire assignments through cross-module routing

question_text: In Static Timing Analysis (STA) software workflows using tools like Synopsys PrimeTime or Cadence Tempus, what file format is primarily utilized to input operational clock frequencies, input delays, and output load criteria?

Option_B: SDC (Synopsys Design Constraints)
Option_C: LIB (.lib Liberty Format)
Option_D: SDF (Standard Delay Format)

question_text: When running physical verification tools like Siemens Calibre or Cadence Pegasus, what exact checking operation is executed during a Layout vs. Schematic (LVS) verification run?

Option_B: It extracts an electrical netlist from the drawn geometric layout shapes and mathematically compares its connectivity structure against the golden pre-layout structural gate netlist to check for mismatches.

question_text: What core structural violation is caught when an EDA layout tool executes a Design Rule Check (DRC) rule deck?
Option_A: Mismatches between the number of logical gates and layout components.
Option_B: Geometric design parameter violations, such as minimum metal width limits, minimum spacing violations between shapes, or enclosure rules mandated by the specific foundry process nodes.

Option_D: Power supply voltage drop limits across signal nets.

question_text: In a typical digital backend flow, what is the correct order of: (i) Floorplanning, (ii) Clock Tree Synthesis, (iii) Placement, (iv) Routing, (v) Sign-off (DRC/LVS/STA)?
What is an SDC (Synopsys Design Constraints) file, and which tools consume it?

question_text: What is the difference between Synopsys IC Compiler II (ICC2) and Synopsys Design Compiler (DC) in the physical design flow?

Option_B: DC is a logic synthesis tool that produces a gate-level netlist from RTL; ICC2 is a place-and-route tool that takes the netlist and physically implements it on the chip (floorplanning, placement, CTS, routing)

Option_D: ICC2 is used for analog design; DC is used for digital design only

question_text: What does Mentor Calibre (or Synopsys IC Validator) perform, and why is it a mandatory sign-off step?
Option_A: It performs gate-level simulation to verify functional correctness after synthesis
Option_B: It runs DRC and LVS physical verification against foundry rule decks, ensuring the final GDS layout is manufacturable and electrically equivalent to the schematic — mandatory before tape-out

Option_D: It generates the standard-cell library LEF and timing models for use in P&R

question_text: What is Cadence Spectre (or Synopsys HSPICE) used for, and how does it differ from a digital HDL simulator like Synopsys VCS?
Option_A: Spectre/HSPICE and VCS are interchangeable; both simulate transistor-level circuits
Option_B: Spectre/HSPICE are SPICE-based analog/circuit simulators solving transistor equations (accurate V/I waveforms, noise); VCS is an event-driven RTL/gate-level simulator for digital logic — Spectre is slower but highly accurate; VCS is fast but uses simplified logic models
Option_C: Spectre is used for digital post-layout simulation; VCS is used for analog block verification
Option_D: VCS supports mixed-signal simulation; Spectre is limited to DC analysis only

question_text: What is the primary role of a Parasitic Extraction (PEX) tool within the back-end digital IC physical design flow?
Option_A: To remove bad gates from the netlist before tape-out.
Option_B: To calculate and output the parasitic capacitances, resistances, and cross-coupling metrics of physical routing metal wires into standard exchange models (like SPEF) for downstream sign-off timing analysis.
Option_C: To generate test structures that improve manufacturing yields.
Option_D: To increase the overall voltage tolerances of logic transistors.

question_text: During the Place-and-Route (P&R) phase in software like Cadence Innovus or Synopsys IC Compiler II, what is the specific objective of the Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS) step?
Option_A: To generate standard cell layouts from behavioral code.
Option_B: To construct an optimized, balanced network of clock routing paths and buffer structures to distribute the clock signal uniformly, minimizing clock skew and latency across all synchronous sequential components.
Option_C: To establish external wire bond interfaces.
Option_D: To calculate the total leakage currents across the silicon chip substrate.

question_text: How does an unexpected increase in the operating temperature generally impact standard digital CMOS standard cell delays?
Option_A: It speeds up the cells because electron mobility increases at high temperatures.
Option_B: It increases the propagation delay of standard cells due to reduced carrier mobility.
Option_C: It has absolutely zero effect on delays; timing varies only with voltage.
Option_D: It eliminates all leakage current components.

question_text: What is 'IR Drop' in a physical backend context, and how is it classified?
Option_A: The failure of timing paths due to high clock frequencies; classified as setup or hold.
Option_B: The voltage drop across the power network caused by current flow through network resistance; classified as Static or Dynamic.
Option_C: The loss of signal integrity due to substrate noise; classified as capacitive or inductive.
Option_D: The displacement of macros during placement; classified as global or detailed.

question_text: Why is a 'Halo' or 'Placement Blockage' explicitly applied around hard macros during floorplanning?
Option_A: To force routing engines to skip the macro entirely.
Option_B: To prevent the placement engine from placing standard cells too close to the macro edges, ensuring routing channel availability and preventing congestion.
Option_C: To connect the substrate of the macro to external grounding rings.
Option_D: To reduce the manufacturing cost of the silicon masks.

question_text: What is 'Clock Skew' in digital physical designs?
Option_A: The drift of the absolute clock frequency over operating time.
Option_B: The spatial difference in clock signal arrival times at two distinct flip-flop clock pins.
Option_C: The total delay from the phase-locked loop (PLL) to the first buffer.
Option_D: The pulse-width distortion caused by asymmetric rise and fall delays.

question_text: Which type of clock distribution structure is characterized by balanced, symmetric branching to ensure near-zero structural clock skew?
Option_A: Fishbone Clock Grid
Option_B: H-Tree Network
Option_C: Spine Architecture
Option_D: Random Mesh Distribution

question_text: What is the 'Antenna Effect' in sub-micron IC manufacturing and backend design?
Option_A: Wireless cross-talk between the IC and external PCBs.
Option_B: The accumulation of electrostatic charges on long, unconnected metal wires during plasma etching, potentially destroying thin gate oxides.
Option_C: The intentional use of metal layers to transmit radio frequency signals inside the die.
Option_D: The physical bending of metal layers due to excessive thermal stresses.

question_text: How can an antenna violation discovered during routing be successfully resolved?
Option_A: By increasing the clock tree buffer size.
Option_B: By inserting an antenna diode near the gate, or using metal layer hopping (layer routing shifts).
Option_C: By decreasing the supply voltage of the entire core floorplan.
Option_D: By increasing the drive strength of the driving standard cell.

question_text: What is 'Crosstalk Delay' and how does it affect timing paths?
Option_A: Electromagnetic interference from external packages slowing down logic blocks.
Option_B: A modification of wire propagation delay caused by switching activities on adjacent parallel wires due to mutual capacitance coupling.
Option_C: The insertion of extra buffer delay to eliminate setup violations.
Option_D: Thermal noise propagation through common substrate structures.

question_text: If an aggressor net switches in the OPPOSITE direction of a victim net simultaneously, what happens to the victim net's delay?
Option_A: The victim net's delay remains exactly identical.
Option_B: The victim net's delay increases (Crosstalk Max-Delay).
Option_C: The victim net's delay decreases (Crosstalk Min-Delay).
Option_D: The victim net's signal is completely cancelled out permanently.

question_text: What is the purpose of inserting 'Tie-High' and 'Tie-Low' cells into a physical design layout?
Option_A: To route the primary clock tree to high-density modules.
Option_B: To safely connect unused logic gate inputs to VDD or VSS without exposing sensitive thin gate oxides directly to power supply noise surges.
Option_C: To increase the overall leakage current of non-critical functional paths.
Option_D: To serve as decoupling capacitors for macro blocks.

question_text: What is 'Virtual Clock' in STA constraints?
Option_A: A dummy clock used by simulation testbenches that has no frequency.
Option_B: A clock that exists as a timing constraint but is not physically connected to any pin or net inside the current design block.
Option_C: The clock tree root buffer after physical design placement.
Option_D: A clock operating in software to synchronize power states.

question_text: What is the function of 'Decoupling Capacitors' (Decaps) in physical layout design?
Option_A: To isolate clock domains and prevent any data skew.
Option_B: To store localized charge and release it during high-frequency transient switching, minimizing dynamic IR drop.
Option_C: To provide mechanical support to the wire bond pads.
Option_D: To block DC routing currents and pass AC data signals.

question_text: What is the difference between setup time and hold time violation?
Option_A: Setup is a data-before-clock issue; hold is a data-after-clock issue
Option_B: Hold is a data-before-clock issue; setup is a data-after-clock issue
Option_C: Both refer to the same violation in opposite corners
Option_D: Setup applies only to combinational logic

question_text: Which parasitic extraction output is consumed by a static timing analysis tool?
Option_A: SPICE netlist only
Option_B: SPEF (Standard Parasitic Exchange Format)
Option_C: LEF file
Option_D: Liberty (.lib) file

question_text: What is the primary purpose of a power ring in physical design?
Option_A: To reduce clock skew across sequential elements
Option_B: To distribute power uniformly around the core or macros and reduce voltage drop
Option_C: To shield sensitive signal nets from crosstalk
Option_D: To improve placement density of standard cells

question_text: Which routing layer is preferred for power stripes in advanced nodes, and why?
Option_A: Metal 1, because it is closest to transistors
Option_B: Upper metals (M5-M9+), because they have lower sheet resistance and handle more current
Option_C: Local interconnect, because it has the best EM margin
Option_D: Poly, because it runs perpendicular to diffusion

question_text: What is electromigration (EM) and its primary impact?
Option_A: Parasitic coupling between wires causing glitches
Option_B: Migration of metal atoms due to current density, leading to open or short circuits
Option_C: Leakage current increasing with temperature
Option_D: Oxide breakdown under high voltage

question_text: In CTS, what is 'useful skew'?
Option_A: Clock skew introduced unintentionally by buffering
Option_B: Intentionally introduced clock skew to borrow time for critical paths
Option_C: Skew between scan and functional clocks
Option_D: Skew caused by PVT variation

question_text: What is a multi-corner multi-mode (MCMM) analysis?
Option_A: Testing the chip in multiple EDA tools
Option_B: Running STA across different operating modes and PVT corners simultaneously
Option_C: A routing constraint for multiple metal layers
Option_D: Verifying DRC at multiple process generations

question_text: What information is primarily stored in a Liberty (.lib) file?
Option_A: Cell geometry and pin locations
Option_B: Timing, power, and functional characteristics of standard cells
Option_C: Routing parasitics extracted from layout
Option_D: Floorplanning constraints and placement rules

question_text: Why is shielding applied to critical signal nets during routing?
Option_A: To reduce leakage current through the signal wire
Option_B: To minimize crosstalk noise by placing power or ground wires adjacent to the signal
Option_C: To decrease via resistance in multi-layer routing
Option_D: To improve electromigration reliability of the net

question_text: What does 'legalization' mean during placement?
Option_A: Signing off the design with the foundry
Option_B: Resolving overlapping cells and snapping them to legal grid sites
Option_C: Removing DRC violations post-routing
Option_D: Verifying power intent compliance

question_text: What does routing congestion indicate during physical design?
Option_A: Excessive power consumption in the design
Option_B: Routing demand exceeds the available routing resources in a region
Option_C: Clock skew exceeds allowable limits
Option_D: Standard cells are violating setup timing

question_text: In physical verification, what is the difference between LVS 'short' and 'open'?
Option_A: Short = net missing connection; Open = two nets incorrectly merged
Option_B: Short = two nets incorrectly merged in layout; Open = a required connection is missing
Option_C: Both represent the same class of error
Option_D: Short applies to poly; Open applies to metal layers only

question_text: What is 'metal fill' and why is it required?
Option_A: Filling unused routing tracks with signal wires
Option_B: Inserting floating metal shapes to meet density rules for CMP planarity
Option_C: Adding power stripes after routing
Option_D: Replacing defective vias with redundant ones

question_text: What does positive setup slack indicate in Static Timing Analysis?
Option_A: The data path violates hold requirements
Option_B: Additional timing margin exists before a setup violation occurs
Option_C: The clock tree contains excessive skew
Option_D: The path has routing congestion issues

question_text: What is the significance of the 'critical path' in timing analysis?
Option_A: The routing path with the most congestion
Option_B: The combinational logic path with the worst (least positive or most negative) setup slack
Option_C: The longest metal wire in the design
Option_D: The path from primary input to primary output with most logic stages

question_text: What does 'double patterning' refer to in advanced lithography?
Option_A: Printing two metal layers simultaneously
Option_B: Splitting a dense layout into two separate lithography exposures to achieve sub-wavelength features
Option_C: Using two different light wavelengths for exposure
Option_D: Applying two resist layers for better etch selectivity

question_text: What is a 'logical equivalence check' (LEC) and at what stage is it performed?
Option_A: Checking that synthesized/placed netlist is functionally identical to the RTL
Option_B: Verifying clock tree buffers are symmetric
Option_C: Confirming that standard cells match their SPICE models
Option_D: Running DRC on the final GDS

question_text: What is 'pin access' in the context of detailed routing?
Option_A: The ability to probe internal nets during test
Option_B: The routability of reaching a cell pin from the routing grid without violating DRC
Option_C: Power pin connections defined in the LEF
Option_D: I/O pad ring accessibility

question_text: What is a 'well tap cell' and why is it needed in CMOS layouts?
Option_A: A cell that samples internal voltages for testing
Option_B: A cell that ties the N-well to VDD and P-substrate to VSS to prevent latch-up
Option_C: A filler cell with added decoupling capacitance
Option_D: A cell that sets the body bias voltage

question_text: Which timing corner is typically used to check for hold violations?
Option_A: Slow-slow (SS) corner, low temperature
Option_B: Fast-fast (FF) corner, low temperature
Option_C: Typical-typical (TT) corner, 25 degrees C
Option_D: Slow-fast (SF) corner, high temperature

question_text: Which cell type is explicitly inserted into layout rows to ensure continuous n-well and substrate bias connectivity across standard cell rows?
Option_A: Clock Buffers
Option_B: Filler Cells
Option_C: Antenna Diodes
Option_D: Level Shifters

question_text: What is On-Chip Variation (OCV) in timing analysis?
Option_A: Voltage drop across power rails during switching
Option_B: Delay variation caused by manufacturing and environmental differences across the chip
Option_C: Crosstalk noise between adjacent nets
Option_D: Variations in clock frequency due to PLL drift

question_text: Why are multiple vias often inserted between metal layers in critical nets?
Option_A: To reduce cell placement density
Option_B: To lower resistance and improve electromigration reliability
Option_C: To minimize clock skew
Option_D: To reduce substrate noise coupling

question_text: If a design experiences a critical HOLD timing violation right before tape-out, what is the standard, safest physical method to resolve it?
Option_A: Increase the operational frequency of the master clock.
Option_B: Insert delay buffers or redundant inverters along the failing data path.
Option_C: Upsize the driving register's power configuration to speed it up.
Option_D: Completely re-route the entire chip using fewer metal layers.

question_text: What is the physical meaning of 'Density Rules' in foundry DRC decks?
Option_A: The maximum number of standard cells allowed per square millimeter.
Option_B: The percentage of metal area relative to total layer area across a step-window, ensuring uniform chemical-mechanical polishing (CMP).
Option_C: The total number of vias per net segment.
Option_D: The concentration of dopants injected into the transistor channels.

question_text: Which file format contains geometric, structural layout information of standard cells and macros without detailing internal transistor schematics?
Option_A: SPICE Netlist
Option_B: LEF (Library Exchange Format)
Option_C: SDC (Synopsys Design Constraints)
Option_D: LIB (.lib format)

question_text: What is the primary role of 'Global Routing' within the overall routing pipeline?
Option_A: To assign specific geometric coordinates and via positions to every single wire tracking.
Option_B: To divide the layout into a grid zone map and assign net topologies to global routing bins to optimize global congestion.
Option_C: To verify layout compliance against the factory DRC handbook.
Option_D: To fix all localized setup and hold timing failures.

question_text: What does clock uncertainty represent in Static Timing Analysis (STA)?
Option_A: Variations in logic gate functionality
Option_B: Timing margin accounting for clock jitter and skew variations
Option_C: Delay introduced by routing congestion
Option_D: Voltage fluctuations in the power grid
